title: Ship Creek monster story adds to city fishery's lore (6-7-2002)
summary: The Fish Was A Freak -- a Ship Creek king salmon unlike any Rudy Ferland had ever seen. "I figure it weighed 60 pounds, 3 ounces," said Ferland, a salty cuss whose in-your-face East Coast accent seems a natural part of this "On the Waterfront" atmosphere of loading docks and longshoremen. The weight, unfortunately, is an estimate because the 52-inch behemoth literally broke the scale at Ferland's Riverside Adventures tackle shop located on the urban fishery's north bank. "My scale goes to 50 pounds and (the salmon) just spun that needle around and popped it," he said. Ferland used the fish's length to compute the weight. He said it was landed a little over a week ago, on May 28. "A guy from Whittier caught it," Ferland added. What? Someone left the heart of some of Alaska's finest sportfis…

title: Cook Inlet off Anchor Point: Fish there, but tides are a bear (5-26-2005)
summary: ANCHOR POINT -- The biggest halibut of the day struck around 9:30 Saturday morning, just as the action seemed to be tapering and the slack tide had begun to turn. Skipper Greg Markson's six anglers were reeling up to try a new spot when the fish, lurking 170 feet below the charter boat Miss Renee, left its lair on the bottom of Cook Inlet to hit the swiftly retreating herring and octopus bait. Heads turned when the lucky angler cried out, his stout deep-sea rod bent radically over the gunnel, 100-pound-test line peeling in jerks and tugs off his heavy saltwater reel. It was enough to give Markson second thoughts. "Maybe we'll stick around a while longer," he announced as his client strained against the heavy fish. Anglers gearing up to spend Memorial Day weekend fishing the tides and strea…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: The worst day fishing in Alaska beats most else RON WILMOT COMMENT Published: October 8, 2006 Last Modified: October 8, 2006 at 04:26 AM Every time the impending darkness of Alaska's fall weather gets me down, I find a story that makes me appreciate living here. Check out this New York Times story from earlier last week -- "After a Seven-Year Ban, Salmon Fishing Returns to Maine." In 1999, according to the article, Maine closed all of its rivers for fishing for wild Atlantic salmon. The population in the famed Penobscot River had dropped to 530 fish. Five hundred and thirty fish in one of the last wild Atlantic salmon streams in America? How does that happen? Dams. Logging. Pollution. Though it is not mentioned in the article, overfishing probably played a role too. The same factors have d…

title: One more way to catch a king (9-7-2004)
summary: Willow Creek -- When the king salmon struck, Dave Dunivan of Wasilla yanked his rod tip up and leaned back. He reeled furiously, gained some line, then the rod lurched forward and the open-faced reel screeched. The angler dug his feet into the gravel and squared his shoulders, braking the fish's run and causing it to wallow briefly in the current. The salmon thrashed on the surface, showing scales that gleamed like stainless steel sequins. A nearby angler offered a net and J.C. Murray of Eagle River, who appeared on the bank just in time to watch the action, grabbed it and stepped into the river. After a couple more short runs, Dunivan led the fish in close and Murray scooped it up. "Forty pounds!" Murray declared as he lifted the salmon from the water. No one argued. The king was deep-bod…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: SnoBear provides the ultimate in comfortable winter travel ICE FISHING: For only $34,500 you can carry whole family in warmth, comfort. By MELISSA DeVAUGHN Anchorage Daily News Published: October 1, 2006 Last Modified: October 1, 2006 at 06:41 AM The very things that can make winter fishing a challenge in Alaska -- extreme cold, limited daylight hours and unstable ice -- are a problem no more. If you have $34,500 to spend. Valley entrepreneurs Mark Braaten and Lee Raiter introduced something called the SnoBear at last weekend's Winter Recreation and Travel Show. Part snowmobile, part boat and part recreational vehicle, the space-age looking SnoBear is the cushiest ice-fishing tent an angler could ever want. It drives like a car, sleeps two in flip-down bunks, contains outlets for attaching…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Beaver damage in neighborhood Urban neighbors balance joy of wildlife with protection of their property By MELISSA DeVAUGHN Anchorage Daily News Published: October 15, 2006 Last Modified: October 15, 2006 at 05:11 AM In a quiet East Anchorage neighborhood of condominiums and single-family homes with neatly trimmed yards, a colony of beavers is quickly becoming unpopular. Day after day these creatures glide along the north end of the neighborhood's man-made Reflection Lake, snipping brush, dragging trash and downing trees in the never-ending task of damming the lake. Biologist Rick Sinnott knows better than to be anthropomorphic when it comes to the rodents. But this time of year, as busy beavers prepare for the onset of winter and begin making a nuisance of themselves -- not only at Reflec…

title: Bad weather's best weather for waterfowlers
summary: PORTAGE -- Sweaty and tired, breathable waders pulled down around his waist to let in the cool of 50-degree September air, Pete Davis stood smiling in sunshine, butchering ducks and cursing the weather. Wind, cold and rain would be preferable, he said. Welcome to the strangely demented world of the waterfowler. Opening day 2006 dawned with white clouds floating in a blue sky. That was fine for the first hour or two, about as long as the naivety of the ducks lasted. Before midday, though, with the marshes echoing a rumble of shotgun blasts, the birds had become wary. Wariness soon gave way to spookiness. Davis and buddy Mark Maves, who'd laboriously dragged a canoe across a half mile of flooded grass to reach a series of canals in the flats between the Twentymile and Portage rivers, watched…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Alaska smarts help woman flee armed kidnappers MEXICO: An experienced hunter. Tammy Griffin escaped into the jungle. By ALEX deMARBAN Anchorage Daily News Published: October 9, 2006 Last Modified: October 16, 2006 at 04:47 PM An Anchorage woman fled on foot in flip-flops through a dark jungle early Friday morning to escape a group who had kidnapped her hours earlier in Zihuatanejo, Mexico. Tammy Griffin, 46, a regional manager for The Hotel Group, slashed her feet during her flight but wasn't seriously hurt, her three children said Sunday. Her husband, Mike Griffin, 52, also of Anchorage, was not seriously injured either, though abductors pistol-whipped him before taking Tammy, intending to hold her for ransom. Tammy -- who stands 5 feet, 4 inches and weighs 120 pounds -- phoned her childr…

title: Bikers test their limits while exploring Yukon Quest trail
summary: High overhead, the aurora borealis rippled a spectacular, multicolored light show across the northern sky. Mike Curiak glanced up and kept pedaling. With the temperature dropping out of sight, he was afraid to stop. It was at least 50 degrees below along the frozen Yukon River, and Curiak couldn't help but wonder what he'd gotten into this time. A mountain bike veteran, the Grand Junction, Colo., cyclist won a 1,100-mile bike race from Knik to Nome over the Iditarod Trail in 2000. This time Curiak had taken off with Anchorage's Pat Irwin in February 2003 to explore the possibility of staging a wilderness mountain bike race over a portion of the colder, more-desolate Yukon Quest International Sled Dog Race route from Fairbanks to Whitehorse, Yukon. Other bikers had said they, too, might joi…

title: 1 of 1 Ron Hoskins of Pennsylvania readies himself for an evening of moose hunting in a blind on the Chena Flood Control…
summary: 1 of 1 Ron Hoskins of Pennsylvania readies himself for an evening of moose hunting in a blind on the Chena Flood Control Project in North Pole early in September. Hoskins, a member of the Paralyzed Veterans of America, was taking part in a special moose hunt. Through a cooperative agreement with the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, the PVA has offered its members, about 30,000 strong, a chance to go moose hunting in Alaska each of the last four years. Photo by SAM HARREL / Fairbanks Daily News-Miner

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Cleanup crews get the pick of the litter RIVERS: "Unique stuff" can be unearthed after the Kenai season. By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: September 7, 2006 Last Modified: September 7, 2006 at 05:26 AM After a summer in which the Kenai River has given much to anglers comes a chance for anglers to give back. The Alaska Flyfishers' annual Kenai River Clean Up is this weekend near Cooper Landing. Volunteers can haul off wads of monofilament line discarded along the Kenai and Russian rivers over the course of the summer, pick up pounds of fishing lead left behind, and discover who knows what else. "We find a lot of unique stuff," said Bob Wrentmore, president of the fly-fishing club. "You probably can't put it in the newspaper. People do strange things down there at night." Pushe…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Crab broker brings buyers to Alaska to see real deal LAINE WELCH FISHERIES Published: September 23, 2006 Last Modified: September 23, 2006 at 05:02 AM KODIAK -- Rob George has had a long love affair with king crab and he is sharing it in an unconventional way. George is co-owner of the Anchorage-based Crab Broker Inc., which sells all kinds of crab from around the world. But his specialty is fresh cooked Alaska king crab. "It's my baby," he said, adding that he was the first to begin brokering fresh-cooked king crab clusters out of Dutch Harbor 14 years ago. George wanted to create a closer working relationship with his customers, and to help them understand the complexities of getting Alaska king crab to their restaurants or retail counters. "I wanted to take them from boat to box -- to s…

title: Training  in the field
summary: On the outside, John Knowles appears to be just like any other teenager. The 14-year-old Wasilla High School freshman is a football player and a big brother. What sets him apart, though, is that he took on responsibility for training his family's springer spaniel, Maddie, as a hunting dog. John's father, Lee Knowles, purchased Maddie with the intent of training her for hunting. Each week he took Maddie and John to a training session with experienced dog handler Maggie Lindsey. Then, Lee was deployed to Afghanistan, and John stepped up to finish the training his father had started. John says he has been working with Maddie for about two years. "His father is proud of him," said John's mother, Tonya Knowles. Tonya Knowles and John's two younger siblings, Jessica, 5, and Conner, 10, gathered …

title: Searchers lose hope for  two climbers
summary: Searchers have given up hope that the two climbers missing on Mount Foraker are alive. "We're no longer searching for two living people," Kris Fister, spokeswoman for Denali National Park, said Sunday. "It's no longer a rescue." When weather allows, and assuming no live search efforts are under way, rescue crews will continue looking for the bodies of Sue Nott, 36, of Vail, Colo., and Karen McNeill, 37, of Canmore, Alberta -- experienced mountaineers who were last seen May 14. The two were climbing a tough and technical route up the 17,400-foot Mount Foraker, southwest of Mount McKinley in Denali National Park. Aerial crews have combed the peaks looking for the pair since June 1. Searchers never saw the women, but they did find a backpack and some gear. "You still hold out this little bit …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Catching the late-season run in Whittier The fishery can be fickle, but time it just right and it's worth the trip By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: September 24, 2006 Last Modified: September 24, 2006 at 05:26 AM Click to enlarge HEAD OF THE BAY -- On a sunny day in mid-September, the setting for stalking Whittier Creek silver salmon is simply idyllic. The waters of Passage Canal are glass flat. The towering, clean, white hulk of the cruise ship Coral Princess looms above the water and blocks any view of the somewhat less attractive community of Whittier. Mountains of gray, bare rock climb above slopes of rich, green spruce trees along the fjord that stretches into the infinity of Prince William Sound. In places along the ridge lines, hanging glaciers and snowfields sparkle …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Above the Arctic Circle, hunter bags a massive moose TALE OF THE TAPE: Chuck Winters of Soldotna likely has one for the record book. By PATRICE KOHL Peninsula Clarion Published: October 8, 2006 Last Modified: October 8, 2006 at 04:24 AM KENAI -- Chuck Winters of Soldotna and a hunting partner from Palmer inched toward a bull moose laying in a northern Alaska marsh early last month. Careful to only walk when gusts of wind masked the sound of their footsteps, the hunters had crept to within 40 feet of the moose when it stood up. Winters' hunting partner, Matt Chambers, swiftly aimed his rifle, pulled the trigger and dropped the animal dead on the spot. Jubilant, Winters turned to congratulate Chambers, but soon realized his excitement had been premature. "I was fixing to give him a high five…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Community effort helps disabled hunt moose FAIRBANKS: Veterans group picks hunters by lottery for the trip north. By TIM MOWRY Fairbanks Daily News-Miner Published: September 27, 2006 Last Modified: September 28, 2006 at 07:06 AM FAIRBANKS -- Ron Hoskins looked pretty much like any other moose hunter in Alaska. Dressed in camouflage from head to toe, he sat in a hunting blind at the edge of the woods on a seepage channel within the Chena River Lakes Flood Control Project, waiting for a bull moose to appear. For Hoskins, who has been confined to a wheelchair for 30 years as a result of a car accident, it was therapeutic. The birds and squirrels didn't know he was paralyzed and neither would a bull moose if it happened to show up within shooting distance. The fact he was in a wheelchair didn…

summary: Bad weather's best weather for waterfowlers IT'S A BLAST: Hunting is best in the worst conditions, unless you've gotten way out of shape. By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: September 10, 2006 Last Modified: September 10, 2006 at 03:18 AM PORTAGE -- Sweaty and tired, breathable waders pulled down around his waist to let in the cool of 50-degree September air, Pete Davis stood smiling in sunshine, butchering ducks and cursing the weather. summary: Training in the field With his father serving overseas, Wasilla teenager takes job of making family spaniel into a hunting dog By MICHELLE STEWART Perfect World Published: September 8, 2006 Last Modified: September 14, 2006 at 03:39 PM On the outside, John Knowles appears to be just like any other teenager. title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Groups, agencies work together to preserve habitat for all species in area Elk island By MELISSA DeVAUGHN Anchorage Daily News Published: September 10, 2006 Last Modified: September 10, 2006 at 03:17 AM Click to enlarge In the deeply forested lands of Afognak Island, fewer than 1,000 Roosevelt elk wander freely, ignorant of the property lines they cross. They ford creeks, climb ridges and wade through kettle ponds. They winter in protected bays that shield them from the snow. They breed and raise their young under the shelter of old-growth Sitka spruce. The elk know nothing of the humans who've worked to protect their habitat. They simply exist, hunted occasionally, photographed rarely, expanding in population during mild years and suffering losses during harsh ones. It is a scenario that …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Autumn migrants stage a subtle show for birders CHALLENGES: Feathered visitors tricky to spot, but rare ones appear. By ELIZABETH MANNING Daily News correspondent Published: September 10, 2006 Last Modified: September 10, 2006 at 03:18 AM Fall migration can be subtle compared to spring migration, posing challenges to birders, Senner said. Many birds don't sport the same bright colors they had in early spring on their way to their breeding grounds. That can make some species difficult to identify. Birds also sing less, making them harder to find. "It's less dramatic, and also less visible to the casual viewer," Senner said. So when does fall migration officially start? That depends partly on the weather. Senner said the birds are extremely responsive to weather changes and can adjust their …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Fisherman holds derby lead, waits anxiously HOMER: Anchorage man filled free seat, hopes to receive about $43,000. By MELISSA DeVAUGHN Anchorage Daily News Published: October 1, 2006 Last Modified: October 1, 2006 at 06:35 AM The 53-year-old recently unemployed Anchorage angler accompanied a friend on a fishing trip to Homer this summer and landed a 341.8-pound monster in the Homer Halibut Derby. He caught the giant flatfish only after replacing an acquaintance who ended up sick and passed his seat on the boat. "I had only planned to fish that first day," Olson said. "But we didn't catch that much that day because the weather was rough. On the second day, it was nice out." Unless some monster of a halibut was hauled from the depths of Kachemak Day in the final days of September -- which ha…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Revelers flock from around the state to cheer Arctic Man daredevils Photos by Photos by BOB HALLINEN • Story by CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: April 16, 2006 Last Modified: April 17, 2006 at 10:43 AM From all over Alaska, people come to the Hoodoo Mountains in April to watch the men and women without fear. There were 17 of them this year. Most strapped skis on their feet, but a fair number opted for snowboards as they slipped into place at the top of a 1,700-foot drop off a 5,800-foot peak near Summit Lake in the Alaska Range. At the bottom, if they made it, they would hook up with a speeding snowmobile that would tow them 2 1/4 miles up a canyon at speeds approaching 90 mph before whip-cracking them over a second snowy summit into a screaming, 1,200-foot descent to the finis…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Kenai Peninsula beetle kill may help boost hunters' odds MOOSE: New growth provides prime habitat for coveted ungulates. By WILL MORROW Peninsula Clarion Published: September 11, 2005 Last Modified: September 11, 2005 at 12:38 AM KENAI -- The rainy, cool weather that has set in over the past couple of weeks can only mean one thing: Time to go hunting. Over the past few years, between 500 and 600 moose have been harvested by Kenai Peninsula hunters each year. Alaska Department of Fish and Game area management biologist Jeff Selinger expects similar numbers this season. "It's usually between 500 and 600, though lately we've been having more toward the 500 range," Selinger said. Part of the reason for the slight decline in harvest numbers has been the gradual decline in quality moose habitat …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: 4 More Guides Cited: Sting Snares UAF Professor By NATALIE PHILLIPS Anchorage Daily News Published: May 21, 2006 Last Modified: May 21, 2006 at 11:47 PM Editor's Note: Story appeared in The Anchorage Daily News, 4/30/1993 Four more hunting guides including a University of Alaska Fairbanks arctic biology professor were indicted by a federal grand jury this week as a part of Operation Brooks Range, a two-year undercover investigation by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service that targeted violators of federal hunting laws. Since December, a total of 12 guides have been charged and eight airplanes valued at nearly $300,000 have been seized. Operation Brooks Range is winding down with these latest arrests, said Tim Eicher, a U.S. Fish and Wildlife law enforcement agent. Were now in the prose…
